publications.title: A structural basis for immunodominant human T cell receptor recognition.
publications.authors: Stewart-Jones GB, McMichael AJ, Bell JI, Stuart DI, Jones EY.
publications.executive_summary: This study determined the crystal structure of the immunodominant anti-influenza V(β)17V(α)10.2 TCR bound to the MP(58-66) peptide presented by HLA-A2, at 1.4 Å resolution. The structure reveals an unusual binding geometry in which the TCR sits nearly orthogonal to the peptide-MHC groove, allowing a conserved arginine from the V(β) CDR3 to insert into a surface notch formed by MP(58-66) itself rather than contacting exposed peptide side chains—a previously undescribed interaction mode. This distinctive binding mechanism explains why V(β)17V(α)10.2 TCRs dominate the CD8+ T cell response to influenza matrix protein in HLA-A2-positive individuals, providing a molecular basis for immunodominant TCR selection in natural infection.

publications.title: The structural dynamics and energetics of an immunodominant T cell receptor are programmed by its Vbeta domain.
publications.authors: Ishizuka J, Stewart-Jones GB, van der Merwe A, Bell JI, McMichael AJ, Jones EY.
publications.executive_summary: The study characterizes the Vbeta17:Valpha10.2 JM22 TCR binding to the influenza matrix peptide (58-66) presented by HLA-A*0201, a public TCR clonotype frequently observed in influenza-infected individuals. Through kinetic, thermodynamic, and structural analyses, the authors identified a binding hotspot within the Vbeta domain, where germline-encoded residues in CDR1, CDR2, and CDR3 drive strong HLA-A2-flu recognition. These structural features explain why Vbeta17-containing TCRs are enriched in the naive repertoire and readily selected during immune responses to influenza. The findings demonstrate how conserved germline sequences can bias TCR usage toward immunodominant clonotypes, providing molecular insight into the paradox of public TCR selection despite the vast diversity of the TCR repertoire.

publications.title: Molecular basis for universal HLA-A*0201-restricted CD8+ T-cell immunity against influenza viruses.
publications.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
publications.authors: Valkenburg SA, Josephs TM, Clemens EB, Grant EJ, Nguyen TH, Wang GC, Price DA, Miller A, Tong SY, Thomas PG, Doherty PC, Rossjohn J, Gras S, Kedzierska K.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined CD8+ T-cell immunity in HLA-A*0201+ individuals responding to the conserved influenza M1 epitope (M158) and compared it to responses against the variable NP418 epitope presented by HLA-B*3501. A dominant public TCR (TRAV27/TRBV19+) was selected across HLA-A*0201+ donors and exhibited cross-recognition of naturally occurring M158 variants through induced-fit molecular mimicry, as revealed by ternary crystal structures of TCR-peptide-MHC complexes. This plasticity explains why the M158 epitope remains conserved across influenza strains and why HLA-A*0201+ individuals mount universal CTL responses despite viral evolution. The findings have broad implications for influenza vaccine design, indicating that inclusion of variant peptides could elicit broader cross-reactive immunity, particularly against epitopes that are underrepresented in naturally primed memory populations.
